prompt:
Integration ticket. We send signed webhook deliveries to partners and we consume a few ourselves, and every receiving codebase has hand-rolled the header check slightly differently — one of them still isn't checking the timestamp at all. I want one small module both sides can vendor: webhook_verify.py, stdlib only.

The scheme is the one Stripe made everybody's default. Each HTTP delivery carries a signature header like `t=1700000300,v1=<hex>`: `t` is the sender's unix timestamp (integer seconds), `v1` is lowercase hex HMAC-SHA256 over the signed payload `"<t>.<body>"` — the timestamp as decimal text, a literal dot, then the raw body bytes — keyed with the endpoint secret (utf-8 encoded str). During secret rotation a sender may attach several v1 entries (one per secret it still signs with), and a receiver may have several secrets configured; if ANY (configured secret, v1 entry) pair matches, the signature check passes.

Module-level helper first: `sign(secret, body, timestamp)` returns the complete header string `t=<timestamp>,v1=<hex>` — our delivery simulator and the partner test rig both import it.

Then `class WebhookVerifier(secrets, tolerance=300, clock=time.time, cache_size=1024)`:
- `secrets`: list of active secret strings (rotation = more than one). Empty list → ValueError right in the constructor.
- `verify(body, header, delivery_id=None)` → a VerifyResult with `.accepted` (bool) and `.reason` (None when accepted, else one of the strings below).

Checks run in a fixed order and the first failure decides the reason — support keeps asking "why was this delivery declined" and I want one unambiguous answer:

1. `"bad_header"` — header is None or empty, any comma-separated item lacks an `=`, `t` is missing/repeated/not an integer, or there is no v1 entry at all. Unknown keys (v0 from the legacy scheme, whatever else senders add) are ignored, not errors. Items may have whitespace around them after the comma split; tolerate it.
2. `"stale_timestamp"` — abs(clock() - t) > tolerance. Exactly at the tolerance still passes, in BOTH directions; sender clocks drift both ways and the window is symmetric on purpose.
3. `"no_matching_signature"` — no (secret, v1) pair matches. Hex comparison is case-insensitive (some SDKs uppercase it), and do the comparison with hmac.compare_digest like every integration guide says.
4. `"duplicate"` — this delivery_id was already accepted once. Only checked when delivery_id is given (callers pass the sender's delivery-id header; some legacy senders don't have one, then we skip this check entirely). Declined deliveries never record their id — a partner who retries the same delivery id after fixing their secret config must get through. The replay cache remembers at most cache_size accepted ids and evicts the oldest-recorded id first when full; an evicted id would be accepted again — documented trade-off, size it generously in prod.

One subtlety worth spelling out: the timestamp is part of the signed payload, so a header whose `t` was swapped for a different in-window value must come out as no_matching_signature — that binding is the whole point of the scheme.

test_webhook_verify.py is the acceptance contract: `python3 test_webhook_verify.py` must pass unmodified. Plain stdlib, no third-party anything.
